name: Avenc de s'Embut
starting_point: Parking near Nuestra Señora de la Paz and Mirador de Na Burguesa restaurant (Génova). Follow the path past the radio antennas toward Coll des Pastors. After the first high-voltage tower (on the left), climb a small hill — the pothole is 90 m off the path at the end of the descent.
equipment: 2 ropes (25 m for entrance shaft, ~60 m for handline), 4-5 protectors, 7-8 carabiners, several slings as deviators
description: Considered perhaps the most beautiful cave in the Serra de na Burguesa. The funnel-shaped entrance leads through the hazardous 'Tub de Ses Pedres' (rock tube — only one person at a time due to rockfall danger) into the spectacular 'Sala de las Columnas' with a 13 m column, forests of stalactites, eccentric formations, and speleothems of various colors. Reaches 95 m depth at the bottom of the column hall.
full_description:
We are before what is perhaps the most beautiful cavity of all those currently known in the Serra de na Burguesa. It is neither the one with the greatest development nor the deepest, as in all of that it is surpassed by the Avenc de l'Infern, located near the Puig d'en Bou with its 800 meters of development and its -132 meters of depth. Where it has nothing to envy any cavity in the area is in its beauty, as I said previously, since in its depths there is no shortage of formations of all types that will not leave us indifferent and where we can dedicate hours to photography.

The topography accompanying this description has been kindly provided by F. Grácia, which improves upon the previous one made in 1977 by J. Damians and J. Fernández, which was little more than a sketch. Miquel Ángel Barceló. ENDINS, 22 (1998).

As its name indicates, the entrance to the pothole is found in a depression in the terrain shaped like a funnel at one end of the doline. The anchor for the rigging will be set up on the wall directly opposite the entrance, where two parabolts with their corresponding plates are already installed, and if we want additional security, a little higher up and to the left on another block of stone there is also a spit installed with its corresponding plate.

As soon as we begin the descent, we will place the first protector to protect ourselves from an obvious friction point even while still on the exterior, and another just inside the entrance a little further down. Just three meters further, a new parabolt already installed will allow us to set up the first rebelay, from which we will begin an aerial descent of about 17 meters that will deposit us on a ramp that will force us to balance like a tightrope walker until we manage to stay upright and climb a small ramp where we can wait calmly for the rest of our companions. We find ourselves in a reasonably well-lit chamber of about 15 x 19 m in which the first formations already appear, with a beautiful parietal flowstone standing out. On a rock at the top of the so-called "Tub de Ses Pedres" (Stone Tube) we will rig the second rope, at the -25 m mark. We will place a deviator on a stalagmite to be able to continue descending.

Before continuing with the description of the descent, something very important must be made clear: the entire descent, as its name indicates, is a kind of channel with the floor in some places covered with stones that, with the passage of the caver, tend to come loose and hurtle down at great speed, potentially endangering the safety of anyone in their path. It is not recommended — rather, under no circumstances should more than one person be using this stretch at a time, as the possibility of "raining" stones is certain.

We will place the third deviator using a sling on a parietal flowstone in a place where there is a small alcove in which only two people can remain safe from falling stones. Therefore, before the arrival of a third person, someone must leave the spot. From here we will use some more deviators, taking care to protect the friction points, until we position ourselves at the top of the "Sala de las Columnas" (Hall of Columns) at approximately the -60 meter mark, from where we can continue moving without any type of support.

Right where the "Tub de Ses Pedres" ends and the "Sala de las Columnas" begins, one can observe above our heads some formations to which a piece of floor flowstone is attached on their underside. This seems to indicate that in past times the floor was at that level and was possibly blocking the passage into the "Sala de las Columnas." Today, however, we can pass freely as the ceiling height is considerable. From the top of the "Sala de las Columnas" where we find ourselves, the view is spectacular, reminding me in a way of the Cova del Mármol, since from above one seems to be in an amphitheater from which the entire hall is surveyed. We will continue descending through the hall, always seeking the most suitable route, while we encounter a thick column affected by the solifluction process with vertical displacement. Not far from there, another column of 13 meters in height delights our eyes. Other formations are affected by the solifluction process at the right end of the hall and in other places, while the ceiling remains covered by a forest of stalactites, some of a very pure white color. On the left side, eccentric formations abound, as well as a large stalagmite and stalactites of different colors, some of them still active. I was able to locate only a single gour in the hall.

Only in the deepest part of the hall does the cavity become horizontal, though on a floor covered with stones and formations that have ended up in this place. Right at this spot there is a small shaft of approximately 5 meters that can be accessed by downclimbing without the use of vertical progression equipment. In it a fine column stands out. At the lowest point, we find ourselves at the -95 meter mark — the maximum depth of the pothole.
